State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95314

November 10, 1976


Dorothy von Beroldingen
1255 Post Street
San Francisco, CA  94109

Re:  Statement of Economic Interests

Dear Ms. von Beroldingen:

     We have received the Schedule 721-F which you sub-
mitted to supplement your Statement of Economic Interests and
your accompanying request for an exemption from the requirement
of the Political Reform Act that the identity of certain of
your law clients be disclosed. To assist us in reviewing your
request for an exemption, we would appreciate your providing
us with the following additional information:

     1. You state that your representation of Client #3 is
        a matter of public record, but that the amount of
        the fee is not. Keeping in mind that you are not
        required to disclose the exact amount of the fee you
        received but, instead, need only disclose the fact
        that your share of the fee was $1,000 or more, please
        state how the disclosure of that fact could prejudice
        the tax cases and civil action now pending.

     2. You state that disclosure of your representation of
        Client #6 could reveal corporate confidential infor-
        mation to competitors. Please provide further detail
        of how this might occur (using fictional names, pro-
        ducts or services, if necessary).

     3. You state that your representation of Client #7 is
        a matter of public record, but the amount of the fee
        is not. As in the case of Client #3, please state
        how disclosure of the fact that Client #7 paid you a
        fee of $1,000 or more, when your representation of
        the client is already publicly known, could prejudice
        protection of trade secrets or lead to unwarranted
        tax investigations.

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or white, with only very faint marks visible at the top and a small dot or mark in the middle-lower portion of the page. The content is not legible enough to transcribe any specific text with confidence.

[illegible]

Dorothy Von B--- ---angen
Page Two
November 10, 1976


4. You state that your representation of Clients numbered
1, 2, 4, 5 and 6 is not a matter of public record and
that disclosure of their names on your Statement of
Economic Interests could lead to an IRS or FTB investi-
gation and audit that might not otherwise occur. Are
there any facts upon which you base this belief other
than the ones set forth in your statement: that you are
listed in various directories as a tax specialist and
are known in San Francisco as a "tax attorney," and that
the public will therefore naturally conclude that clients
who consult you must have tax problems?

5. Your statement appears to indicate that each of the
seven clients for whom you seek an exemption are clients
of your sole practice and not clients of a partnership
or professional corporation with which you are associated.
Please confirm whether that is correct.

Thank you for your cooperation. If you have any ques-
tions, please don't hesitate to call me.

Sincerely,

Michael J. Baker
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

The image appears to be a blank or nearly blank page with only a few small marks or spots visible at the top and center of the page. There is no clearly legible text to transcribe.

Statement of Dorothy von Beroldingen, Attorney at Law
(Supplemental Schedule 721-F, Interim Economic Disclosure Statement)

During the period covered by the above statement, the undersigned
received fees in excess of $1000.00 from eight (8) clients, disclosure
of the name and address of one of which has been recorded on Supplemental
Schedule 721-F attached, since my representation as well as the fees
received are a matter of public record and do not require protection
against disclosure.

As to the remaining seven (7) clients, the names of these clients
have been withheld on the grounds that such disclosure would violate
the attorney-client privilege, could lead to investigation and/or
audit by the Internal Revenue Service or Franchise Tax Board which
would not otherwise occur, or could lead to the disclosure of trade
secrets and other confidential information to competitors of the
client.

Additionally, objection is hereby made to disclosure, based upon the
fact that Government Code section 87207 was not intended to reach
confidential information unless the source of income from the client
in question might be affected materially by decisions of the Board
of Supervisors of which I am a member, in a manner different from
the public generally or a significant segment thereof.

The objection is further based upon the constitutional guarantee of
equal protection under the law, which would be violated if the
privilege of non-disclosure is granted to doctors but denied to
attorneys.

The attorney-client privilege herein asserted is particularly
applicable and controlling in this case. I am a certified specialist
in taxation law, having received my certificate of specialization
from the California Board of Legal Specialization of the State Bar
of California on November 30,1973. My name is so listed in the State
Bar's legal specialists roster, and it is so listed in the San Francisco
telephone directory advertising, and it is a matter of common knowledge in San Francisco that I am a "tax attorney". Regardless of
the nature of the problems for which my clients may seek my counsel,
there is a natural conclusion drawn by the public that those who
consult me must have tax problems -- possibly of a fraudulent or
criminal nature, or at the very least, that some version of the tax
laws prompted them to select me as counsel. This is in fact true
in some cases, but there would be nothing in the listing of a name
to distinguish those clients with totally legitimate tax concerns
from those who had possibly overstepped the legal bounds and were
facing serious charges as a result.

Thus, by analogy to the patient whose name is protected by the
physician-patient privilege where its disclosure would also reveal
the nature of the treatment received, I submit that the names of

The image appears to be mostly blank/white with only a few small circular elements visible at the top of the page. There is no readable text content to transcribe.

-2-

my clients are similarly protected. (See Marcus v. Sup. Ct.,
18 C.A. 3d 22, Ascheman v. Sup. Ct., 254 C.A 2d 506).

In order to enable the Commission to properly evaluate the arguments
herein set forth, I have listed seven (7) clients whose names
are withheld, identifying them by numbers only, but describing the
nature of the services rendered which, in addition to the overall
prejudice that would result from public knowledge that had
consulted a tax attorney, could lead to other adverse effects.
I have discussed this matter with each of the clients, and they
would be very much opposed to disclosure of their names, some of
them indicating that should this be required, they would have to
seek other counsel. See Exhibit A attached.

It is submitted that in enacting Government Code Sec. 87207, it was
never the legislative intent to jeopardize the only real source of
income available to a member of the San Francisco Board of Super-
visors, by reducing those of us who are attorneys to professional
pariahs, unable to attract clients because of the clients' natural
and legally protected desire for privacy.

Finally, it is noteworthy that under no circumstances could the
representation of a client in tax matters in any way affect those
decisions I am called upon to make as a member of the San Francisco
Board of Supervisors. As stated in my Economic Disclosure Statement
filed February 2, 1976, I do not accept as clients, even for tax
advice, those who have or might have any dealings with the City and
County of San Francisco, or whose business or property interests
require decisions or action by any agency of the City and County,
other than the payment of their property taxes.

Reference is made to Findings of Judge Broaddus in Hays v. Wood,
No. 36319, Mendocino County Superior Court, page 4, lines 22-25:

"9. Government Code Section 87207(b) is reasonably
limited to intrude into only those personal
financial affairs of WOOD which might be affected
by his official actions as a Ukiah City Council-
man." (Emphasis added.)

Favorable action in accordance with the foregoing is respectfully
requested.

Dated at San Francisco, California, this 4 day of November, 1976.

[signature]
Dorothy von Beroldingen

[The image appears to be a mostly blank page with only two circular objects visible at the top, possibly page holes or binding marks. The rest of the page is white/blank with no discernible text to transcribe.]

EXHIBIT A

Client #1: Tax advice re failure to file; no public record.

Client #2: Tax and immigration advice; neither representation nor
         fees paid are a matter of public record.

Client #3: Tax advice and related litigation. Fact of representa-
         tion is a matter of public record but fees are not;
         possible internal Revenue Service and Franchise Tax
         Board litigation involved, and disclosure could be
         prejudicial to both the tax cases and the civil action
         which is of public record.

Client #4: Tax advice and tax planning for professional client.
         No public record of representation or fees paid.
         Disclosure of identity of client could lead to IRS and
         FTB investigation and audit that would not otherwise
         occur.

Client #5: Tax advice and tax planning. No public record of re-
         presentation or fees paid. Disclosure of identity of
         client could lead to IRS and FTB investigation and audit
         that would not otherwise occur.

Client #6: Financial and tax planning for individual and close
         corporation. No public record of representation or
         fees paid. Disclosure of identity of client could lead
         to IRS and FTB investigation and audit of individual
         and corporation that would not otherwise occur; may
         also reveal corporate confidential information to
         competitors.

Client #7: Tax and corporate planning; protection of trade secrets,
         litigation still pending; prevention of foreclosure and
         corporate takeover. Pending litigation still pending; disclosure
         could lead to unwarranted tax investigations and could
         prejudice protection of trade secrets. Representation
         in some of the matters are of public record, but fees
         paid are not.

[The image appears to be a mostly blank page with very minimal visible text or markings. There are a few small dots or marks visible near the top of the page, but no clearly legible text content to transcribe.]

LAW OFFICES
DOROTHY VON BEROLDINGEN          Ghirardelli Square
1255 POST STREET                 4th Floor . Chocolate Bldg.
SUITE 703                        900 North Point St.
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94109  San Francisco 94109

December 13, 1976

Michael J. Baker
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street Building
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, Ca. 95804

Re: Statement of Economic Interests

Dear Mr. Baker:

Your letter of November 10, 1976 has not been given the prompt
attention I would have preferred, largely because I have been in
the process of moving my law office.

In this regard, you have asked for further information concerning
the nature of my law practice. Since January 11, 1955, when I was
admitted to practice in California, I have been a sole practitioner
except for some three years when I was a member of a partnership
(1955-1959 and 1968-1969). Commencing with January 1, 1977, I will
be a member of an association of lawyers, which is neither a pro-
fessional corporation nor a partnership; the association will prac-
tice at the above new address.

Having given careful thought to the questions raised in your most
recent communication I have concluded that any further information
concerning my clients and the nature of the services rendered to
them which results in fees of $1000 or more would be of no assist-
ance in resolving the basic issues presented in this matter.

I would reiterate that Finding No. 9 made by Judge Broaddus in
Haws v. Wood is particularly applicable to me, namely that the
requirements of Government Code Section 87207(b) are limited to
intrude into only those personal financial affairs which might
be affected by my official actions as a member of the San Francisco
Board of Supervisors. None of my personal financial affairs result-
ing from payment of fees by the seven clients itemized in my state-
ment attached to the Supplemental Schedule 721-F submitted on
November 4, 1976 could in any way be affected by my official actions,
and I believe it is clear on the face of that statement that this
is demonstrably true.

Four of those clients do not even do business in the City and
County of San Francisco, nor do they reside there, and of the three
who reside within the City and County of San Francisco, there is no
possibility that any of my actions as a Supervisors could be even
remotely related to their legal problems or to them personally
--except for fixing of the tax rate which affects the entire

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or extremely faded, with only very faint marks visible. The text content is not legible enough to transcribe accurately. There appear to be some circular marks or stamps at the top of the page and possibly some extremely faint text throughout, but it cannot be read with sufficient clarity to transcribe verbatim.

Michael J. Baker                 -2-


city and in no way distinguishes them from other taxpayers.

     Far beyond the question of exposure of these clients to government
investigation or other inconvenience that could be occasioned by
disclosure of their identities is the fundamental issue of their
right to privacy as well as my own obligation to protect their pri-
vacy and professional confidentiality.  Not one of them is willing
to consent to the disclosure you are seeking and several of them
have indicated that should such disclosure occur they would change
attorneys, thus effectively depriving me of my basic right to
engage in the practice of law unless I surrender my equally basic
right to serve the public as a legislator as I was elected to do.

     It is my sincere belief that the requirements of Proposition 9,
to the extent that they amend the previously controlling "Moscow
statute" of 1973, which required no more than identification of the
source of income, namely, the business address of the law office
that received the fees, are unconstitutional in that they are over-
broad and discriminatory.

     They are overbroad in that they exceed the requirements necessary to
the valid governmental purpose which they were designed to implement:
There is no necessity for inquiry into those financial affairs which
can reasonably be expected to have a direct or indirect effect on
my activities as a public official, nor is there any safeguard built
into the requirements to protect me against the consequences of
violation of a professional confidence.

     They are discriminatory in that an equal partner in a law firm of
more than ten attorneys can escape the reporting requirements entirely,
and the discrimination against attorneys in general's patent in that
an accountant, doctor, dentists, engineer, architect or businessman
escapes reporting the identity of all clients or customers except for
those who patrony him more than $10,000, as opposed to the $1000 thresh-
hold established for attorneys who practice alone or in a firm of
less than ten attorneys.

     It is my understanding that Hays v. Wood is presently on appeal, and
I would urge that all further action on cases of similar import be
stayed until the results of that appeal, and what will undoubtedly
become a decision of the Supreme Court of the State of California,
on these very fundamental issues regarding the constitutionality
of existing law are known.  It would appear that any other course
can only entail needless time, effort and expense for all concerned.
My colleagues on the Board of Supervisors as well as those on the
Golden Gate Bridge, Highway and Transportation District Board of
Directors who are attorneys affected by these precise questions
have concurred in this opinion, and appropriate resolutions have been

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or extremely faded, with only very faint marks visible that are not legible enough to transcribe with confidence. There are what appear to be two small curved marks near the top of the page and some extremely faint text in the middle section, but the content is not readable enough to transcribe accurately.

[illegible]

Michael J. Baker                -3-


adopted in principle by each Board, the exact forms of which are
now being prepared by counsel for these respective bodies. Copies
will be furnished to you as soon as they are available.

While I believe there is an important public interest to be protected
in requiring disclosure of possible conflicts of interest of public
officials, I have to conclude, based not only on my own opinion but
that of two other attorneys whom I have consulted in this matter,
that there are ways to accomplish this public purpose that do not
compet attorney-public officials to risk disciplinary action by the
State Bar or civil liability to clients whose confidential relation-
ship with their attorneys has been breached by compliance with the
disclosure laws.

I am sure that you appreciate the gravity of the situation for me
and those similarly situated, and will recognize that the within
arguments are being asserted in good faith and not for purposes of
delay or in an attempt to evade compliance.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
DvB/ms                                 Dorothy/von Beroldingen

embedding.qa_text: November 10, 1976 Dorothy von Beroldingen 1255 Post Street San Francisco, CA 94109 Re: Statement of Economic Interests Dear Ms. von Beroldingen: We have received the Schedule 721-F which you submitted to supplement your Statement of Economic Interests and your accompanying request for an exemption from the requirement of the Political Reform Act that the identity of certain of your law clients be disclosed. To assist us in reviewing your request for an exemption, we would appreciate your providing us with the following additional information: 1. You state that your representation of Client #3 is a matter of public record, but that the amount of the fee is not. Keeping in mind that you are not required to disclose the exact amount of the fee you received but, instead, need only disclose the fact that your share of the fee was $1,000 or more, please state how the disclosure of that fact could prejudice the tax cases and civil action now pending. 2. You state that disclosure of your representation of Client #6 could reveal corporate confidential information to competitors. Please provide further detail of how this might occur (using fictional names, products or services, if necessary). 3. You state that your representation of Client #7 is a matter of public record, but the amount of the fee is not. As in the case of Client #3, please state how disclosure of the fact that Client #7 paid you a fee of $1,000 or more, when your representation of the client is already publicly known, could prejudice protection of trade secrets or lead to unwarranted tax investigations. The image you provided is completely white with two small circular marks in the upper left and upper right corners. There is no visible text, numbers, or other content to analyze or transcribe. If you have another page or another image, please provide it for further analysis. 4. You state that your representation of Clients numbered 1, 2, 4, 5 and 6 is not a matter of public record and that disclosure of their names on your Statement of Economic Interests could lead to an IRS or FTB investigation and audit that might not otherwise occur. Are there any facts upon which you base this belief other than the ones set forth in your statement: that you are listed in various directories as a tax specialist and are known in San Francisco as a "tax attorney," and that the public will therefore naturally conclude that clients who consult you must have tax problems? 5. Your statement appears to indicate that each of the seven clients for whom you seek an exemption are clients of your sole practice and not clients of a partnership or professional corporation with which you are associated. Please confirm whether that is correct. Thank you for your cooperation. If you have any questions, please don't hesitate to call me. Sincerely, Michael J.
